context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Markets and Marketization

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How are market institutions different from the state? What do you need to know about market institutions if you want to work with them? How can you work jointly within different types of organizations and what are the challenges you might face by doing that? How does marketization — the process of creating markets — happen and what mistakes should it avoid?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I need help w/ this reflection on markets vs the state. I’m kinda stuck on how to explain marketization and the challenges of working across orgs.
exchanges.tutor: Good, let's work through it together. Before we dive in — what have you written or thought about so far? Even rough ideas are fine. Share what you've got and we'll build from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified two specific areas they're stuck on. Rather than giving them scaffolding on both at once, I should start by assessing what they already know. I'll ask them to share what they have so far, which lets me understand their current thinking and guide from there using the Socratic method.

exchanges.student: So far I just have that the state makes rules and markets respond to incentives, but that feels too simple lol. I’m also guessing marketization is when stuff gets privatized, but idk if that’s the whole idea.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct is right — there's more to it. Let me ask you this: if the state makes rules, what does that mean for markets? Can markets even *exist* without the state doing something first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a basic but not wrong starting point. Rather than validating or correcting directly, I should use Socratic questioning to push them to deepen their own thinking. Their instinct that it's 'too simple' is correct and worth building on. I'll ask one focused question to help them refine their understanding of the state vs. markets distinction, since that's foundational to the whole piece. I won't address marketization yet - one thing at a time.

exchanges.student: I think not really? like markets need property rights, contracts, maybe courts, so the state kinda sets the stage first.
exchanges.tutor: Good thinking. So if the state sets the stage, what does that tell you about the *nature* of markets themselves — how do they operate differently from the state once that stage is set?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a solid leap - they've identified that markets are embedded in institutional frameworks created by the state. This is a key insight. I should affirm the direction without giving the answer, and now push them to think about what makes markets *different* from the state despite this dependency, which gets at the heart of the first prompt question.

exchanges.student: Maybe markets coordinate thru prices and competition, while the state coordinates thru authority, law, and policy. I’m not sure how to say that without making it sound too binary tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's a real tension worth sitting with. What kinds of organizations or actors might exist *between* pure markets and the state? Think about the development context specifically.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and showing nuance by worrying about the binary framing. This is worth encouraging. I should push them to think about what lies in between or complicates the binary, which will naturally lead them toward the 'working jointly within different types of organizations' part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: Maybe NGOs, donor agencies, public-private partnerships, cooperatives, even informal networks? tbh that’s where I get fuzzy about who has power.
exchanges.tutor: Good list. Now think practically: if you're a planner trying to work with, say, both a government ministry and a private firm on the same project, what kinds of friction or conflict might come up between them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is identifying relevant actors and connecting it to the question of power, which is a key course theme. Rather than explaining the power dynamics for them, I should push them to think about what specific challenges arise when you try to coordinate across these different types of organizations - this directly addresses the 'challenges you might face' part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: I’d expect diff goals maybe—public welfare vs profit, plus diff timelines, accountability, and who gets to decide stuff. Also trust issues maybe, esp if donors or outside actors are involved.
exchanges.tutor: Those are solid points — hold onto them for your writing. Now let's go back to marketization. You said it might just be privatization — but given everything you've said about what markets *need* to function, do you think privatization alone is enough to create a working market?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is generating strong, relevant ideas about coordination challenges. This is good material for their reflection. I should now pivot to marketization since they've built enough foundation, and push them to refine their earlier guess that it's just privatization - they already sensed it was incomplete.

exchanges.student: Nah, probably not. If u just privatize without regulation, institutions, or access, it could just make a private monopoly or exclusion worse.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — so what does that tell you about what marketization actually *requires* to work? And what are the mistakes a planner should specifically watch out for?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a critical insight about marketization - that it requires more than just privatization. This directly connects to the 'mistakes marketization should avoid' part of the prompt. I should push them to articulate what the full process of marketization actually involves, and what specific mistakes follow from getting it wrong.

exchanges.student: I think it requires building the rules + institutions around exchange, not just selling state stuff off. Mistakes would be assuming markets are “natural,” ignoring inequality/power, and copying reforms without local context.
exchanges.tutor: You've got strong ideas covering all parts of the prompt now. It's time to stop talking about it and start writing. Take everything we discussed and put it into your draft — in your own words. Come back with a version and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now developed strong ideas across all parts of the prompt. They've covered state vs. markets, what you need to know to work with market institutions, cross-organizational challenges, and marketization. We've had about 7 exchanges and they have enough material to write. I should now push them to start integrating all of this into their actual written piece rather than continuing to discuss, as per the redundancy rule and the 'never write for the student' rule.

exchanges.student: Yeah fair, I’ll draft it now. Could u maybe help me w/ a super quick outline first so I don’t ramble?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I should do for you — the outline is part of your thinking process. But you already *have* the pieces from our conversation. What order would *you* put them in to answer the prompt logically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to produce an outline for them, which would cross the line into doing their work for them. My role is to guide, not to write or structure content on their behalf. I should decline clearly but redirect them constructively by asking them to try building their own outline based on what we've discussed.

exchanges.student: Maybe: markets vs state first, then what planners need to know about market institutions, then cross-org collaboration/challenges, then marketization + mistakes? That order seems kinda clean I think.
exchanges.tutor: That tracks well with the prompt. Now go write it — come back with your draft when it's ready.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced their own outline that closely mirrors the structure of the prompt. This is good and shows they've internalized the discussion. I should affirm it briefly and send them off to write - no more scaffolding needed at this point.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently using questions to draw out student reasoning rather than providing answers. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. Every turn used targeted questions to guide the student to generate their own ideas (e.g., Turns 2–8). In Turn 9, the tutor explicitly refused to provide an outline and redirected the student to do their own thinking. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student responses (e.g., affirming 'good thinking' and 'good list' before advancing), and each question was calibrated to what the student had just said, showing appropriate scaffolding. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback implicitly by redirecting the student to their own reasoning and flagging when it was time to move from discussion to drafting (Turn 8). 2.1–2.2: The conversation progressed logically through all prompt components without repetition or drift. 3.1–3.2: Responses were concise, interactive, and appropriately supportive without being evaluative or grade-like.
